heroOverview: Dehydration occurs when fluid losses exceed intake, reducing total body water by 1% or more and impairing the normal volume and osmolality of blood and tissue. Even a 2% reduction in body weight from fluid loss measurably impairs cognitive performance, physical endurance, and cardiovascular efficiency; a loss of 10% or more is life-threatening. Globally, diarrhoeal diseases — the leading cause of dehydration — kill approximately 1.6 million children under five each year (WHO 2023), making dehydration one of the most preventable causes of death worldwide. In adults, gastroenteritis, heat exposure, fever, uncontrolled diabetes, and inadequate fluid intake during illness are the most common triggers. Prompt oral rehydration corrects mild-to-moderate dehydration in most patients without hospital admission.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects people of all ages; diarrhoeal dehydration kills approximately 1.6 million children under five annually worldwide (WHO 2023); up to 40% of elderly people admitted to hospital from care homes show evidence of chronic underhydration
keyStats.demographics: Children under 5 and adults over 65 are at highest risk of severe outcomes; athletes and outdoor workers face high exposure risk
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal peak: infants and young children (diarrhoeal illness) and adults over 65 (impaired thirst perception and polypharmacy)
keyStats.globalCases: Hundreds of millions of episodes annually; WHO estimates over 1.7 billion cases of childhood diarrhoea with dehydration each year

definition: Dehydration (ICD-10: E86.0) is a state of negative fluid balance in which total body water is reduced below the level needed for normal physiological function. The body is approximately 60% water in adult men and 50–55% in adult women; the percentage is higher in infants (up to 75%) and lower in obese individuals, which partly explains why these groups are more vulnerable. Dehydration is classified by severity — mild (1–3% body-weight loss), moderate (3–8%), and severe (above 8%) — and by tonicity: isotonic dehydration (proportional loss of water and sodium, the most common form from diarrhoea and vomiting), hypertonic or hypernatraemic dehydration (water loss exceeds sodium loss, causing high serum sodium; seen in fever, sweating, and diabetes insipidus), and hypotonic or hyponatraemic dehydration (sodium loss exceeds water loss, seen in prolonged exercise with plain water replacement or Addison's disease). The regulation of fluid balance involves thirst perception in the hypothalamus, ADH secretion from the posterior pituitary, aldosterone from the adrenal cortex, and renal handling in the collecting ducts; impairment of any of these mechanisms predisposes to dehydration. The condition is managed across primary care, paediatrics, emergency medicine, and endocrinology depending on the cause.

typesClassification.description: Water and sodium are lost in roughly equal proportions, keeping serum sodium normal; caused by vomiting, diarrhoea, burns, and haemorrhage. This is the most common form and is most effectively replaced with oral rehydration solution (ORS).

Free water is lost in excess of sodium, raising serum sodium above 145 mmol/L; common in infants fed high-solute formula, elderly individuals with impaired thirst, and people with diabetes insipidus or uncontrolled type 1 or type 2 diabetes.

Sodium losses exceed water losses, dropping serum sodium below 135 mmol/L; seen in prolonged vigorous exercise with excessive plain-water replacement, Addison's disease, and severe diarrhoea with hypotonic fluid replacement.

Inadequate fluid intake despite access to water, most common in the elderly (where thirst perception is blunted), frail patients, very young children, and people with cognitive impairment who cannot request fluids.

primarySymptoms: Thirst — the first and most reliable symptom of mild dehydration, triggered by a 1–2% rise in serum osmolality detected by hypothalamic osmoreceptors; blunted in the elderly, making this sign unreliable in older adults.

Dark yellow or amber urine — the simplest practical indicator; normal hydration produces pale straw-coloured urine, while deep yellow to brown urine signals concentrated urine from ADH-driven water retention.
Dry mouth, dry lips, and reduced saliva production — the mucous membranes lose their normal moist sheen, producing a sticky sensation and difficulty swallowing.
Reduced urine output — the kidneys respond to falling circulating volume by reducing urine production; an adult producing less than 0.5 mL/kg/hour (roughly less than 400 mL/day) is oliguric.
Headache — a common early symptom, reflecting reduced intracranial fluid volume and cerebral hypoperfusion; even 1–2% body-water loss is sufficient to cause headache in susceptible individuals.

Fatigue and reduced physical performance — cardiac output falls as blood volume contracts, reducing oxygen delivery to muscles; endurance, strength, and reaction time all deteriorate measurably at 2% dehydration.

Dizziness and light-headedness, especially on standing (orthostatic hypotension) — intravascular volume depletion reduces venous return, and the baroreceptor-mediated compensatory response may be insufficient in the elderly.

Tachycardia — the heart increases rate to maintain cardiac output as stroke volume falls with declining preload; a resting pulse above 100 bpm in a symptomatic patient suggests at least moderate dehydration.

Skin with reduced turgor — when gently pinched on the forearm or abdomen, the skin tent remains for more than 2 seconds; a specific sign in children but less reliable in elderly patients whose skin elasticity is intrinsically reduced.
In infants: sunken anterior fontanelle, no tears when crying, very dry nappies over more than 8 hours — signs of significant volume depletion requiring prompt assessment.
Muscle cramps — electrolyte losses alongside fluid depletion (particularly sodium, potassium, and magnesium) produce painful cramps during or after exercise or illness.

Confusion and altered mental status — moderate-to-severe dehydration reduces cerebral perfusion and worsens electrolyte disturbances; new confusion in an elderly person is a classic presentation of dehydration.
earlyWarningSigns: Urine that is noticeably darker than usual on the first morning void of the day.
Going more than 6 hours without urinating in a warm environment.
A mild headache starting in the afternoon after inadequate fluid intake.
Slight dry mouth and feeling of thirst during or after exercise, fever, or illness.
emergencySigns: No urine output for more than 8 hours in an adult, or for 6 hours in a child, despite attempting oral fluids.
Rapid weak pulse, very low blood pressure, and skin that is cold and mottled — signs of hypovolaemic shock requiring immediate IV fluid resuscitation.
Unconsciousness, extreme confusion, or fitting in the context of a fluid-loss illness or heat exposure.
Sunken eyes and dry mouth in an infant who is floppy and unresponsive — severe paediatric dehydration is a paediatric emergency.
Bloody diarrhoea or vomiting blood alongside dehydration signs — suggests a serious underlying cause requiring urgent investigation.
Severe muscle weakness or paralysis with dehydration, which may signal dangerous electrolyte disturbance (hyperkalaemia or severe hyponatraemia).

causes.description: The most common cause globally; acute gastrointestinal infection produces watery stool and vomiting that can together exceed 10–20 litres of fluid loss per day in severe cases (e.g., cholera); even a mild bout of gastroenteritis can cause 2–4 litres of loss over 24 hours in an adult.

In the elderly, hypothalamic osmoreceptors become less sensitive with age, blunting the thirst drive even when serum osmolality rises; in children, dependence on carers to provide fluids can lead to underhydration during illness or heat; in workers or athletes, fluid access or social norms may suppress drinking.

Sweat rates in unacclimatised adults can reach 1–2 litres per hour during heavy exertion in hot conditions; without replacement, cumulative losses produce progressive dehydration and electrolyte depletion, ultimately contributing to heat exhaustion and heat stroke if untreated.

Hyperglycaemia exceeding the renal glucose threshold (approximately 10 mmol/L) produces an osmotic diuresis that can drive urine output above 5 litres per day; diabetic ketoacidosis combines this massive fluid loss with metabolic acidosis and is a common precipitant of severe dehydration in young people with type 1 diabetes.

Body temperature elevation increases insensible water losses by approximately 10 mL/kg per degree Celsius above 37°C; breathing through the mouth during respiratory illness further increases respiratory vapour loss; combined, a child with 40°C fever and rapid breathing can lose 500–1,000 mL/day extra beyond normal losses.

Loop diuretics (furosemide) and thiazides increase renal water and salt excretion; in frail elderly patients or in those who do not compensate with increased drinking, these drugs predictably cause dehydration, hyponatraemia, and electrolyte depletion.

Major burn injuries destroy the skin barrier, allowing massive transcutaneous fluid evaporation; the Parkland formula estimates fluid replacement needs in burns, which can exceed 4 mL/kg per percentage body-surface-area burned in the first 24 hours.

riskFactors.description: Infants and toddlers have a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio increasing evaporative losses, a greater total-body-water percentage making volume swings more impactful, and total dependence on carers for fluid access; they account for the majority of dehydration deaths globally.

Ageing blunts hypothalamic osmoreceptor sensitivity, reduces baseline kidney concentrating ability, and often reduces mobility and fluid access; frailty and polypharmacy compound the risk. Dehydration in the elderly is strongly associated with confusion, falls, urinary tract infections, and acute kidney injury.

Medications that increase fluid output through the kidney or bowel require proactive compensation with increased oral intake; risk is highest in patients who are not counselled about this at prescription.
Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia produces enormous fluid losses; patients with HbA1c above 75 mmol/mol are at high risk of dehydration episodes, particularly during intercurrent illness.

riskFactors.factor: Hot environment or strenuous outdoor work
riskFactors.description: Ambient temperatures above 35°C and high humidity exponentially increase sweat rates; occupational exposure without access to cool fluid breaks is the primary driver of heat-related dehydration and heat stroke.

Acute gastroenteritis carries high risk in settings with limited access to oral rehydration salts or healthcare; contaminated water supplies and poor sanitation perpetuate episodes of severe diarrhoeal dehydration in low-income settings.

People who cannot communicate thirst or reach a drink — those with dementia, severe stroke, or profound physical disability — depend entirely on carers for proactive fluid provision; institutional dehydration in care homes reflects gaps in hydration monitoring.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 with gastroenteritis — the highest-risk group globally for dehydration mortality
Adults over 65, particularly those with dementia or limited mobility in care settings
Outdoor and agricultural workers in hot climates
Athletes engaged in prolonged endurance sport without structured hydration plans
Patients with uncontrolled type 1 or type 2 diabetes
People in low-income countries with limited access to oral rehydration salts and safe water
Hospitalised patients who are nil by mouth, sedated, or not monitored for fluid balance
diagnosisOverview: Dehydration is largely a clinical diagnosis confirmed by a targeted history, examination findings, and in moderate-to-severe cases, blood tests. History focuses on the duration and volume of fluid losses (vomiting frequency, diarrhoea stool count, urine output), access to fluids, and symptoms. The classic clinical signs — dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes, tachycardia, and orthostatic hypotension — correlate with severity: mild dehydration (1–3% body-weight loss) may show only thirst and dark urine; moderate (3–8%) adds tachycardia and reduced turgor; severe (above 8%) produces hypotension, altered consciousness, and cold extremities. In children, the WHO clinical dehydration scale (DHAKA/Integrated Management of Childhood Illness scheme) uses skin pinch recoil, eye appearance, mucous membranes, and general appearance to classify mild, some, or severe dehydration. Blood tests in moderate-to-severe cases typically show a raised haematocrit and haemoglobin (haemoconcentration), elevated serum sodium (hypertonic dehydration), raised urea and creatinine (pre-renal azotaemia), and decreased urinary sodium. Serum electrolytes are essential when oral rehydration is failing or when vomiting has been prolonged, because hyponatraemia or hypokalaemia may complicate or drive the clinical picture. Urinalysis shows dark, high-specific-gravity (above 1.020) urine with elevated osmolality (above 800 mOsm/kg) in most dehydrated patients who retain renal concentrating ability.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic tool; identifies the cause, severity grade, and physiological consequences of dehydration without waiting for laboratory results.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician reviews symptoms, measures pulse, blood pressure lying and standing, checks mucous membranes and skin turgor, and records recent body weight change if available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies electrolyte disturbances (hypernatraemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia), acute kidney injury (raised creatinine and urea), and acid-base disorders (metabolic alkalosis from vomiting or acidosis from diarrhoea).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; results available in 1–4 hours in an emergency setting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Haemoconcentration — a raised haematocrit and haemoglobin — confirms volume depletion in the absence of anaemia; it falls back toward normal as fluid is replaced.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same blood draw as electrolytes; no additional procedure needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Concentrated, dark urine with specific gravity above 1.020 and osmolality above 800 mOsm/kg confirms the kidneys are appropriately conserving water in response to volume depletion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urine sample is dipped or sent to the laboratory; results in minutes to hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine sodium and fractional excretion of sodium (FENa)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Urine sodium below 20 mmol/L and FENa below 1% confirm pre-renal azotaemia from volume depletion, distinguishing it from intrinsic kidney disease where urine sodium is high.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A spot urine and blood sample collected at the same time; a simple calculation done in the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies metabolic acidosis (diarrhoeal loss of bicarbonate) or metabolic alkalosis (vomiting-related chloride and hydrogen loss) and assesses lactate as a marker of tissue perfusion in severe cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample from a vein or fingertip; results on a bedside analyser within minutes.

treatmentOverview: The mainstay of dehydration treatment is fluid replacement matched to the type, severity, and ongoing rate of loss. Oral rehydration is preferred whenever the patient is alert, not vomiting persistently, and haemodynamically stable. The WHO oral rehydration solution (ORS) — 75 mmol/L sodium, 20 g/L glucose, and 20 mmol/L potassium — harnesses glucose-coupled sodium cotransport in the gut and can replace fluid as effectively as intravenous therapy in mild-to-moderate dehydration; it is the cornerstone of global diarrhoeal disease management and has saved an estimated 50 million lives since its widespread introduction. In adults with mild-to-moderate dehydration from gastroenteritis, 200–400 mL of ORS per episode of vomiting or diarrhoea, taken in small sips, replaces losses; commercial sports drinks contain less sodium than ORS and are acceptable for exercise-related dehydration but not for illness. Intravenous fluid therapy is indicated for severe dehydration, haemodynamic instability, persistent vomiting, altered consciousness, or when oral intake is contraindicated. Standard initial adult IV resuscitation is 250–500 mL of 0.9% saline or a balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's solution, Plasma-Lyte) bolus over 15–20 minutes, repeated until the pulse and blood pressure normalise; a reassessment framework (heart rate, blood pressure, urine output, and serum electrolytes at 1–4-hour intervals) guides ongoing replacement. Maintenance fluid requirements after initial resuscitation are calculated from baseline daily needs (approximately 30–35 mL/kg/day) plus estimated ongoing losses. Electrolyte replacement runs alongside fluid — potassium at 20–40 mmol per litre of IV fluid when hypokalaemia is present, with oral potassium supplementation continued on discharge. Nasogastric ORS delivery is a valuable intermediate option for children who cannot take adequate oral fluids but do not require IV access. Hypernatraemic dehydration requires more cautious correction — falling serum sodium faster than 0.5 mmol/L/hour risks cerebral oedema; hypotonic fluids (0.45% saline or 5% glucose) are used and the deficit replaced over 48 hours. Hyponatraemic dehydration from exercise is treated with small-volume hypertonic saline only in the presence of severe symptoms; asymptomatic cases are corrected with isotonic or moderately hypotonic fluid.

medicalTreatments.name: WHO oral rehydration solution (ORS)
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for mild-to-moderate dehydration from any gastroenteric cause; the glucose in ORS drives sodium co-transport via the SGLT1 cotransporter, bypassing the secretory diarrhoea mechanism and absorbing water efficiently even during active diarrhoea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: As effective as IV therapy for mild-to-moderate dehydration in children in randomised trials; reduces inpatient admission rates by approximately 50% when used early. Reduces diarrhoeal mortality in children by over 90% compared with pre-ORS era outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous isotonic crystalloid (0.9% saline or Hartmann's solution)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for severe dehydration, haemodynamic instability, or vomiting precluding oral intake; 250–500 mL boluses titrated to haemodynamic response, followed by structured replacement of estimated deficit and ongoing losses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reverses haemodynamic instability and haemoconcentration in the great majority of patients when volume is matched to calculated deficit; balanced crystalloids (Hartmann's) are associated with less hyperchloraemic acidosis than 0.9% saline in large trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Nasogastric ORS administration
medicalTreatments.description: Delivers ORS at 25 mL/kg/hour via a nasogastric tube in children who cannot drink adequately but do not require IV access; recommended by NICE as an alternative to IV therapy in children with moderate dehydration who are not severely unwell.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves comparable rehydration outcomes to IV therapy in moderate dehydration with less procedural discomfort and fewer electrolyte disturbances in paediatric studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Electrolyte replacement (oral and IV potassium, sodium)
medicalTreatments.description: Potassium and sodium losses during gastroenteritis, sweating, or diuretic use must be replaced alongside water; potassium is added to IV fluids (20–40 mmol/L) when serum K+ is below 3.5 mmol/L, and continued orally after discharge with dietary sources or potassium chloride supplements.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects hypokalaemia and hyponatraemia in virtually all patients when the dose is matched to the measured deficit and ongoing losses.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiemetics (ondansetron)
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of ondansetron 4–8 mg (children: 0.15 mg/kg) reduces vomiting frequency by approximately 50%, improving the feasibility of ORS administration and halving the rate of IV cannulation in paediatric gastroenteritis randomised trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces IV fluid use in paediatric gastroenteritis by about 50% when given as an adjunct to ORS; not routinely recommended as an adult antiemetic for mild viral gastroenteritis but is used for moderate-to-severe vomiting.

alternativeTreatments.name: Coconut water
alternativeTreatments.description: Naturally contains potassium and some sodium; acceptable for mild exercise-related fluid replacement but contains insufficient sodium (about 25 mg/100 mL versus 90 mg/100 mL in ORS) for illness-related dehydration.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ice chips and ice lollies
alternativeTreatments.description: Useful for persistent nausea or vomiting to deliver small volumes of fluid palatably; particularly effective in children who refuse to drink but will accept an ice lolly made from ORS.

whySeeSpecialist: Most mild-to-moderate dehydration is managed in primary care or at home. Specialist assessment is indicated when dehydration is recurrent without an obvious explanation (suggestingdiabetes insipidus, Addison's disease, or a renal concentrating defect), when severe electrolyte disturbances accompany the dehydration, when dehydration occurs in a patient with heart failure or renal impairment (where fluid replacement requires careful titration), or when a child is severely unwell with persistent signs despite initial ORS therapy.
doctorSelectionGuide: For recurrent or unexplained dehydration in adults, an endocrinologist can investigate diabetes insipidus, Addison's disease, and other hormonal causes of excess fluid loss. A nephrologist manages dehydration complicated by acute kidney injury, hypernatraemia, or severe electrolyte disturbance. Paediatric emergency medicine specialists should assess severely dehydrated children. For dehydration in athletes, a sports medicine physician with expertise in exercise physiology can provide personalised hydration protocols.
preventionStrategies: Adopt a structured hydration habit: drink approximately 30–35 mL/kg body weight of water daily as a baseline, adjusting upward for exercise, hot weather, fever, or diarrhoea.
Monitor urine colour; pale straw yellow indicates good hydration, while dark yellow or amber signals the need to drink more.
For elderly individuals in care settings, implement scheduled fluid rounds offering 150–200 mL every 2 hours during waking hours; do not rely solely on self-reported thirst.
Begin oral rehydration early in any gastroenteritis illness — start ORS within the first episode of vomiting or diarrhoea before significant losses accumulate.

Acclimatise gradually to hot environments over 7–14 days before undertaking heavy outdoor work or sport; acclimatised individuals produce larger volumes of more dilute sweat and have a lower threshold for initiating sweating.

Ensure people with type 1 or type 2 diabetes have a sick-day management plan that includes clear guidance to increase fluid intake and seek care early when blood glucose rises above 15 mmol/L during illness.
Review diuretic prescriptions annually in elderly patients; the lowest effective dose reduces dehydration risk.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a water bottle and drink regularly throughout the day rather than large volumes infrequently.
Increase fluid intake proactively during hot weather — aim for an extra 500 mL for every hour of strenuous outdoor activity.
During illness with vomiting or diarrhoea, switch to sips of ORS rather than large volumes of plain water, which can worsen hyponatraemia.
Avoid alcohol during or immediately after exercise or in hot conditions — alcohol is a diuretic that worsens fluid balance.
Eat fruit and vegetables with high water content (watermelon, cucumber, oranges) as dietary fluid sources alongside drinks.
Monitor morning body weight daily if you are an athlete or have a condition that increases fluid loss — a drop of 1 kg or more from baseline warrants increased fluid intake.

recommended: Water as the primary hydration source — aim for pale urine as a simple guide to adequacy
Oral rehydration solution (commercial sachets or homemade: 1 litre water, 6 teaspoons sugar, half a teaspoon salt) during diarrhoea and vomiting illness
Potassium-rich foods — bananas, sweet potato, yoghurt, lentils — to replace electrolyte losses after gastroenteritis
Diluted fruit juice or clear broths as supplementary palatability options for people who struggle to drink plain water
Water-rich foods — soups, watermelon, cucumber, celery — especially for elderly people with low beverage intake
avoid: Alcohol during or after significant fluid loss episodes — it suppresses ADH, increasing renal water loss
Very sugary drinks such as full-sugar cola or concentrated juice, which can worsen osmotic diarrhoea and delay electrolyte replacement
Plain water as the sole fluid replacement in prolonged vomiting or severe diarrhoea — without sodium, risk of hyponatraemia rises
Caffeine in large quantities during active dehydration, as it has a mild diuretic effect that increases renal water excretion

exerciseGuidelines: Maintain hydration as a priority before, during, and after exercise. Pre-hydrate with 500 mL of water in the 2 hours before prolonged or outdoor exercise. During exercise lasting more than 60 minutes, drink 150–250 mL every 15–20 minutes; include sodium (sports drinks or ORS) in sessions lasting more than 90 minutes to prevent exercise-associated hyponatraemia from excessive plain-water intake. After exercise, aim to replace 150% of the estimated sweat loss (roughly 1.5 kg fluid per 1 kg body-weight lost) over the following 4–6 hours. Active dehydration with any haemodynamic symptom (dizziness, syncope, chest pain) requires stopping immediately and seeking medical attention.
dailyManagement: Check urine colour first thing each morning; adjust fluid intake upward if it is darker than pale straw.
Set a daily fluid goal appropriate for your weight and activity level; use a marked water bottle to track intake.
Recognise early warning signs — headache, dry mouth, dark urine — and respond immediately with fluid.
Keep ORS sachets at home if you have young children or a condition (diabetes, inflammatory bowel disease) that makes you prone to fluid-loss episodes.
If taking diuretics, ask your pharmacist or doctor what fluid intake is recommended and whether dose adjustment is needed during illness or heatwaves.
For elderly family members or care home residents: offer drinks proactively at meals and between meals; do not wait for them to ask.
prognosis: Mild-to-moderate dehydration corrected with oral rehydration or IV fluids has an excellent prognosis, with full recovery within hours to a day. Severe dehydration, once treated in hospital, resolves without lasting sequelae in the great majority of previously healthy patients. The prognosis worsens significantly when dehydration is complicated by acute kidney injury (most cases of pre-renal AKI reverse within 24–48 hours of rehydration, but a small proportion progress to intrinsic renal damage), electrolyte crises (severe hypernatraemia carries a 10–15% mortality in hospitalised patients, partly from cerebral oedema during treatment), or when dehydration is a manifestation of an underlying disease (diabetic ketoacidosis, bowel obstruction, sepsis) that itself carries significant mortality. In children under 5 in low-resource settings, access to ORS is the principal determinant of outcome; countries with strong ORS distribution programmes have reduced diarrhoeal mortality by 70–90% over the past 40 years.
recoveryTimeline: Mild dehydration from exercise or brief vomiting resolves within 2–4 hours of replacing estimated fluid deficit. Moderate dehydration from a bout of gastroenteritis typically resolves within 24 hours of starting ORS. Severe dehydration requiring IV therapy usually stabilises haemodynamically within 1–2 hours of initial resuscitation; full electrolyte correction and renal function normalisation take 24–48 hours. Hypernatraemic dehydration is intentionally corrected more slowly over 48 hours to prevent cerebral oedema.
complications: Acute kidney injury — reduced renal perfusion from volume depletion causes pre-renal azotaemia, manifest as rising creatinine and urea; most reverses within 24–48 hours of adequate rehydration, but prolonged severe dehydration can cause tubular necrosis.
Hypovolaemic shock — loss of more than 20–25% of circulating volume produces hypotension, tachycardia, and poor perfusion of vital organs; a medical emergency requiring urgent IV fluid resuscitation.

Electrolyte disturbances — hypernatraemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, and hyperchloraemic acidosis can each arise from or be worsened by dehydration, and some (severe hyponatraemia, hypernatraemia) carry risk of seizure, brain herniation, or death if corrected too rapidly.

Heat stroke — severe dehydration in a hot environment impairs the sweating mechanism, allowing core body temperature to rise above 40°C; a life-threatening emergency with organ damage and mortality above 10% even with treatment.

Thromboembolic events — haemoconcentration from dehydration increases blood viscosity and promotes venous thrombus formation, particularly deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism in hospitalised elderly patients.

Urinary tract infection — concentrated, static urine in a dehydrated patient provides a culture medium for bacterial colonisation; adequate hydration is a well-established preventive measure against UTI.

supportResources.name: WHO — Diarrhoeal Disease Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: WHO overview of diarrhoeal disease, including the role of dehydration, oral rehydration therapy, and global burden, especially in children.

supportResources.name: NHS — Dehydration
supportResources.description: Plain-English guidance from the UK National Health Service on recognising and treating dehydration in adults and children.

supportResources.name: CDC — Making Oral Rehydration Solution
supportResources.description: Practical guidance on preparing and using oral rehydration therapy from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

sources.title: WHO — Oral rehydration salts: production of the new ORS. Document WHO/FCH/CAH/06.1, 2006
Spandorfer PR et al. Oral versus intravenous rehydration of moderately dehydrated children: a randomized, controlled trial. Pediatrics, 2005
NICE NG29 — Intravenous fluid therapy in children and young people in hospital, 2020
NICE CG174 — Intravenous fluid therapy in adults in hospital, 2017 update
Popkin BM, D'Anci KE, Rosenberg IH. Water, hydration, and health. Nutrition Reviews, 2010
Mange K et al. Language guiding therapy: the case of dehydration versus volume depletion. Annals of Internal Medicine, 1997

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypernatremia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypernatraemia is an electrolyte diagnosis (serum sodium above 145 mmol/L) that frequently coexists with dehydration but is distinct; dehydration can occur with normal, high, or low sodium, and hypernatraemia requires different fluid replacement strategies than isotonic dehydration.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyponatremia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyponatraemia is low serum sodium and can paradoxically occur with dehydration (sodium losses exceed water losses), overhydration (dilutional), or normal fluid status (SIADH); the clinical picture and serum sodium together distinguish these states.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heat stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heat stroke is a specific hyperthermic emergency (core temperature above 40°C) with neurological dysfunction; severe dehydration from heat often precedes it, but heat stroke has additional direct organ damage beyond volume depletion and requires active cooling, not just rehydration.

relatedConditions.name: Hypernatremia
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypertonic dehydration produces hypernatraemia when free water losses exceed sodium losses; the two diagnoses co-exist and guide the type of replacement fluid used.

relatedConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
relatedConditions.relevance: Pre-renal AKI is the most common form of acute kidney injury and is almost exclusively caused by volume depletion; it reverses with adequate rehydration in most cases.

relatedConditions.name: Heat stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe dehydration impairs the sweating mechanism and is a major contributor to heat stroke; aggressive early hydration is the primary prevention strategy in heat-related illness.

relatedConditions.name: Sepsis
relatedConditions.relevance: Sepsis causes massive vasodilation and capillary leak, producing a distributive dehydration-like state requiring large-volume IV fluid resuscitation; distinguishing septic shock from hypovolaemic dehydration guides vasopressor use.

faqs.question: What is dehydration?
faqs.answer: Dehydration occurs when your body loses more fluid than it takes in, reducing total body water below the level needed for normal function. Even a 2% reduction in body weight from fluid loss impairs energy, concentration, and physical performance.

faqs.question: What are the signs of dehydration?
faqs.answer: Early signs include thirst, dark yellow urine, dry mouth, and headache. Moderate dehydration adds fatigue, dizziness, and rapid heartbeat. Severe dehydration causes confusion, very low urine output, cold skin, and collapse — a medical emergency.

faqs.question: How do I know if I am dehydrated?
faqs.answer: Check your urine colour: pale straw means well hydrated; dark yellow or amber means drink more. Other clues are thirst, headache, fatigue, and dry lips. A drop in body weight of 1% or more after exercise also indicates fluid loss.

faqs.question: What causes dehydration?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are diarrhoea and vomiting from gastroenteritis, not drinking enough fluid in hot weather or during illness, heavy sweating during exercise, fever, and uncontrolled diabetes causing osmotic fluid loss through the kidneys.

faqs.question: How much water should I drink per day?
faqs.answer: A useful guideline for adults is 30–35 mL per kg of body weight daily — roughly 2–2.5 litres for a 70 kg adult — as a baseline. You need more when exercising, in hot weather, or during illness. Pale urine is the simplest sign of adequate hydration.

faqs.question: What is oral rehydration solution (ORS)?
faqs.answer: ORS is a precisely balanced mixture of glucose, sodium, and potassium in water. The glucose drives sodium absorption through a gut transporter that remains active even during diarrhoea. WHO ORS has saved tens of millions of lives and is as effective as IV fluids for mild-to-moderate dehydration.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for dehydration?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if you cannot keep any fluid down for more than 6–8 hours, if urine output stops for more than 8 hours, if there is confusion or extreme weakness, if a child becomes floppy or drowsy, or if you feel faint and have a racing heart at rest.

faqs.question: How is dehydration treated?
faqs.answer: Mild-to-moderate dehydration is treated with oral rehydration solution — small sips taken frequently. Severe dehydration needs intravenous fluids (saline) in hospital. Electrolytes lost with the fluid, particularly sodium and potassium, are replaced alongside water.

faqs.question: Is dehydration dangerous for children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children — especially under 5 — dehydrate faster than adults and are at higher risk of serious complications. Worldwide, diarrhoeal dehydration is one of the leading causes of child deaths. Start oral rehydration solution early in any vomiting or diarrhoea illness and seek care if the child is drowsy or not improving.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause a headache?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even a 1–2% reduction in body water is enough to trigger a headache in many people, thought to reflect slight reduction in cerebrospinal fluid volume and cerebral blood flow. Drinking 500 mL of water often relieves a dehydration headache within 30–60 minutes.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause kidney damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe or prolonged dehydration reduces blood flow to the kidneys and causes acute kidney injury (pre-renal AKI), which shows up as rising creatinine on a blood test. In most people this reverses completely with rehydration, but prolonged severe dehydration can cause lasting tubular damage.

faqs.question: Does coffee and tea cause dehydration?
faqs.answer: Caffeine has a mild diuretic effect, but at normal intake levels — up to 4 cups of tea or coffee daily — the fluid in the drink more than compensates. The NHS and EFSA both count tea and coffee toward daily fluid intake. Large caffeine quantities during active dehydration are worth avoiding.

faqs.question: Can you drink too much water?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drinking very large volumes of plain water — particularly during prolonged exercise — dilutes blood sodium below safe levels (hyponatraemia), causing nausea, headache, confusion, and in severe cases, seizures and coma. Matching fluid to sweat rate and using electrolyte drinks during events over 90 minutes prevents this.

faqs.question: How do I rehydrate quickly?
faqs.answer: The fastest safe approach is to drink 500–1,000 mL of oral rehydration solution over 1–2 hours in small regular sips. Avoid drinking a large single volume, which may provoke nausea. For very rapid rehydration in hospital, IV saline can be infused over 15–30 minutes under monitoring.

faqs.question: What is the best drink for dehydration?
faqs.answer: For illness-related dehydration, oral rehydration solution is the most effective option because it contains the right sodium and glucose ratio to maximise intestinal absorption. For exercise dehydration without illness, water or a diluted isotonic sports drink is appropriate. Plain water is fine for mild everyday dehydration.

faqs.question: Why are the elderly at higher risk of dehydration?
faqs.answer: With age, the thirst mechanism becomes less sensitive, so older adults may not feel thirsty even when their fluid intake is inadequate. The kidneys also become less able to concentrate urine. Mobility limitations and medications (especially diuretics) compound the risk. Proactive regular fluid offering is essential in elderly care.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause constipation?
faqs.answer: Yes. When the body is short of fluid, the colon absorbs more water from stool to compensate, making it hard, dry, and difficult to pass. Adequate daily fluid intake is one of the simplest and most effective strategies for preventing constipation.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause dizziness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reduced blood volume from dehydration lowers the blood pressure that reaches the brain, particularly on standing quickly. This orthostatic hypotension produces a sudden dizzy or light-headed feeling. Drinking fluid and sitting down usually resolves it within a few minutes.

faqs.question: Is it safe to exercise when dehydrated?
faqs.answer: No. Even mild dehydration (2% body weight) reduces endurance, strength, and concentration by a measurable amount and raises core body temperature faster. Exercising while significantly dehydrated risks heat exhaustion or heat stroke. Rehydrate before returning to exercise.

faqs.question: How can I prevent dehydration in hot weather?
faqs.answer: Drink water regularly throughout the day rather than waiting until you are thirsty, especially in temperatures above 30°C. Wear light-coloured breathable clothing, seek shade, avoid strenuous activity in the hottest part of the day (11am–3pm), and increase intake by at least 500 mL for each hour of outdoor activity.

metaDescription: Dehydration causes dark urine, headache and dizziness from fluid loss. Learn symptoms, how to rehydrate, and when to seek hospital care. Doctor-reviewed.
